strands.title: Data Archaeology Phase 2: Archive Metadata and Structure Inventory Complete
strands.content: Cycle 59 identified 14 session files; cycle 60 now has file inventory with turn counts and sizes. This enables assessment of data richness and comparability. FILE INVENTORY (14 sessions, confirmed): BASELINE EXPERIMENTS: • baseline_run1: 57 turns, 457KB (medium-length). • baseline_run2: 100 turns, 391KB (LONGEST conversation). • baseline_run3: 65 turns, 640KB (largest file, indicates rich strand structure). COMPARISON EXPERIMENTS (three-way variant test): • comparison_baseline_run1: 16 turns, 174KB. • comparison_trimmed_run1: 21 turns, 208KB. • comparison_compact_trim_run1: 16 turns, 174KB. INFERRED PATTERN: All three comparison variants are SHORT runs (16-21 turns), suggesting they are test runs or rapid experiments. Size parity (trimmed and compact_trim both 174KB despite different turn counts) is odd; may indicate format differences or metadata variance. OPTIMIZATION EXPERIMENT: • full_optimization_run1: 4 turns, 44KB (VERY SHORT; likely incomplete or testing-only). This is surprising: if full_optimization tested the cycle 55 per-batch and gating architecture, why only 4 turns? Hypothesis: (a) Early termination for testing. (b) Different input (shorter conversation). (c) Debugging run, not representative of optimized performance. IMPACT: full_optimization_run1 may not be suitable for hypothesis testing; treat as exploratory only. SYSTEM PROMPT EXPERIMENTS: • minimal_sysprompt_run1: 4 turns, 3KB (EXTREMELY SHORT; minimal content). • minimal_sysprompt_run2: 4 turns, 3KB (identical to run1). These are not substantial experiments; they are placeholder or test runs. Hypothesis: (a) Minimal system prompt caused early termination or parsing failure. (b) These were debugging runs to test whether projector could run with minimal instructions. (c) Data may be corrupted or incomplete. CRITICAL FINDING: minimal_sysprompt runs are NOT viable for hypothesis testing. They provide no epistemic signal; too short to show strand evolution or meta-recursion patterns. ADDITIONAL VARIANT: • long_compact_trim_run1: 40 turns, 589KB (medium-long, substantial size). This was NOT in cycle 59's initial analysis (possibly discovered during cycle 60 file search). Suggests there may be variants of variants (e.g., compact_trim tested at different scales). TESTING/VERIFICATION EXPERIMENTS: • test_author_run1: 55 turns, 339KB (medium-long; substantial decoder closure test). • test_author_run2: 30 turns, 386KB (medium-short; close in size to run1 despite different turns). • test_author_run3: 32 turns, 261KB (medium-short; consistent with run2). STRONG FINDING: Three test_author runs exist with substantial content (30-55 turns, 260-386KB). These are THE MOST IMPORTANT runs for cycle 60 priority because: (1) They tested decoder closure (external model understanding). (2) They have sufficient length to show strand evolution. (3) Decoder success across variants would provide strong evidence for meta-recursion necessity (or sufficiency). • verify_streaming_fix_run1: 5 turns, 7KB (VERY SHORT; minimal content). Likely testing-only; I/O validation, not substantive experiment. REVISED VIABLE DATASET (for hypothesis testing): HIGH PRIORITY: • baseline_run2 (100 turns) — longest baseline; best for establishing baseline epistemic trajectory. • test_author_run1 (55 turns) — decoder closure test; critical validation. • long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns) — substantial reduced-recursion test; allows comparison to baseline. MEDIUM PRIORITY: • baseline_run1 (57 turns) and baseline_run3 (65 turns) — replication of baseline; assess stability. • test_author_run2 (30 turns) and test_author_run3 (32 turns) — replication of decoder test; assess robustness. LOW PRIORITY (due to brevity; use only for exploratory patterns, not hypothesis testing): • comparison_baseline_run1, comparison_trimmed_run1, comparison_compact_trim_run1 (16-21 turns each) — too short; limited power. • full_optimization_run1 (4 turns) — incomplete; uninformative. • minimal_sysprompt_run1/2 (4 turns, 3KB each) — broken or test runs; no signal. • verify_streaming_fix_run1 (5 turns) — I/O test; not substantive. ASSESSMENT OF ARCHIVE SUFFICIENCY (cycle 60): POSITIVE SIGNALS: (1) Baseline has three runs (57, 100, 65 turns) → replication possible. (2) Test_author has three runs (55, 30, 32 turns) → decoder closure testable across variants. (3) long_compact_trim_run1 is substantial (40 turns) → reduced-recursion hypothesis testable. (4) File sizes suggest rich strand structure (baseline_run3 is 640KB for 65 turns; ~10KB/turn, indicating detailed tensors or metadata). NEGATIVE SIGNALS: (1) Comparison variants are TOO SHORT (16-21 turns) for pattern detection; insufficient power. (2) full_optimization_run1 is incomplete (4 turns); cannot test optimization hypothesis robustly. (3) minimal_sysprompt runs are BROKEN or TRIVIAL (3KB total); no epistemic signal. (4) No clear evidence of RICH-INPUT vs. THIN-INPUT comparison; H3 (context-dependent) may remain untested. (5) Archive does not show a CONTROLLED EXPERIMENTAL MATRIX. Turns vary widely (4 to 100); suggests different input conversations, different termination conditions, or different experimental intents. Comparability is UNCERTAIN. CRITICAL LIMITATION: We do not know IF all runs used the same input conversation or different conversations. If baseline_run1 (57 turns) and baseline_run2 (100 turns) fed different inputs, we cannot directly compare epistemic patterns; differences could be input-driven, not baseline-driven. HYPOTHESIS: Runs with SIMILAR turn counts likely used similar inputs (e.g., baseline_run1 57 turns, baseline_run3 65 turns; test_author_run2 30 turns, test_author_run3 32 turns). Runs with VERY DIFFERENT turn counts may have used different inputs or been terminated early. DECISION POINT FOR CYCLE 60: Archive is PARTIALLY SUFFICIENT. Can test: • H2 (meta-recursion is essential): Compare baseline vs. long_compact_trim_run1. If compact_trim produces lower quality (fewer strands, lower truth values, more declared losses), that supports H2. If compact_trim is indistinguishable from baseline, that refutes H2. • Decoder closure robustness: Analyze test_author_run1/2/3. Did external model understand baseline tensors? Did it understand compact_trim tensors? Success across variants would support meta-recursion utility; failure would suggest it was artifact. Cannot robustly test: • H1 (meta-recursion is artifact): Requires showing that removing meta-recursion has NO COST. Current variants (trimmed, compact_trim) are too short to assess. Would need substantial reduced-recursion runs. • H3 (context-dependent): No clear evidence of RICH-INPUT comparison. Archive may not test input-dependence hypothesis. • H4 (design-choice): Requires showing that alternative designs (e.g., cycle 55's per-batch gating) produce similar results. full_optimization_run1 is too short. NEXT IMMEDIATE ACTIONS (cycle 60, turn 180+): (1) PARSE baseline_run2 (100 turns) first: Extract epistemic trajectories (truth/indeterminacy/falsity per cycle), strand counts, declared losses. Establish baseline pattern. (2) PARSE long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns) second: Extract same metrics. Compare to baseline_run2. Do patterns differ? Does compact_trim show degradation or stability? (3) PARSE test_author_run1 (55 turns) third: Extract decoder closure results if embedded in logs. Did external model understand tensors? (4) If parsing reveals FULL TENSOR CONTENT (not just logs), proceed to deep analysis in cycle 61. If only partial content, assess what can be learned from available data. (5) Communicate findings to cycle 61: Can we test H2 robustly? What about H1, H3, H4? Gap assessment. EPISTEMIC POSTURE (cycle 60): Cautiously optimistic. Archive has 14 sessions; three of them (baseline_run2, long_compact_trim_run1, test_author_run1) are substantial and potentially informative. But archive was NOT DESIGNED for the four hypotheses; it was likely designed for earlier purposes (optimization, verification, debugging). Retrospective analysis carries risk: we may find patterns that SEEM to answer hypotheses but are actually artifacts of uncontrolled input variation. MITIGATION: Before drawing conclusions from patterns, always ask: 'Could this pattern be explained by differences in input conversation, rather than by meta-recursion design?'

key_claims.text: 14-session inventory complete: 3 baseline, 3 comparison, 1 optimization, 2 minimal_sysprompt, 3 test_author, 1 streaming_fix, 1 long_compact_trim
Baseline runs have substantial length (57-100 turns); comparison and optimization variants are SHORT (4-21 turns)
HIGH-PRIORITY VIABLE DATA: baseline_run2 (100 turns), test_author_run1 (55 turns), long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns)
LOW-PRIORITY DATA: comparison variants (16-21 turns), full_optimization (4 turns), minimal_sysprompt (3KB, trivial)
Archive does NOT show controlled matrix: turn counts vary (4-100); unclear if different inputs or early termination
Can test H2 (meta-recursion essential) via baseline vs. compact_trim comparison; cannot robustly test H1, H3, or H4

strands.title: Cycle 60 Actions: baseline_run2 Projected (100 turns, first 15 requested)
strands.content: TURN 180 ACTION: Assistant initiated PROJECTION RUN on baseline_run2 (the longest baseline, 100-turn session) using run_projection pipeline. This is DATA ARCHAEOLOGY EXECUTION: not analyzing pre-existing tensors, but RE-PROJECTING the conversation through the current projector implementation. RATIONALE FOR RE-PROJECTION (not just parsing existing tensors): (1) REPRODUCIBILITY: If baseline_run2 session log is raw conversation (not full tensor output), re-projecting allows us to generate fresh tensor cycles. (2) VERSION CONSISTENCY: Running current projector ensures all comparison runs use the same projector version (eliminates version-confound). (3) FULL TENSOR RECOVERY: If archived session is incomplete or corrupted, re-projection generates complete, structured tensor output. (4) COMPARATIVE BASELINE: First fresh projections will establish baseline epistemic pattern; then we compare against variants. COMMAND STRUCTURE: uv run python -m hamutay.run_projection <input_jsonl> <output_dir> <first_N_turns> Inputs: • input_jsonl: baseline_run2 session file (100 turns, 391KB). • output_dir: /home/tony/projects/hamutay/experiments/baseline_run2 (writes projected tensors to this dir). • first_N_turns: 15 (process first 15 conversation turns; this is SAMPLING, not full run). RATIONALE FOR N=15: (a) Full 100-turn re-projection would be expensive (time, tokens). (b) 15 turns is sufficient to establish baseline epistemic trajectory (show if truth/indeterminacy/falsity change early). (c) Allows rapid comparison across multiple runs (can re-project 3-4 diverse runs with N=15 before incurring large cost). (d) If baseline pattern at turn 15 is clear, can infer full-run pattern (assuming no phase transition mid-conversation). BACKGROUND RISK: Assumption that 'first 15 turns establish pattern' may be WRONG. Epistemic trajectory might shift dramatically at turn 50-100. If so, N=15 sampling is insufficient. MITIGATION: After baseline_run2 analysis, examine a LONGER sample (e.g., first 40 turns of long_compact_trim_run1) to assess if early patterns persist. EXECUTION STATUS (turn 180): Command dispatched asynchronously (timeout: 600,000ms = 10 minutes). Projector runs in background; results will be available at /home/tony/projects/hamutay/experiments/baseline_run2/. EXPECTED OUTPUTS: • Tensor cycles (one per turn or per batch): strands, epistemic values, declared losses, open questions. • Metadata: cycle IDs, turn references, timestamps. • Summaries: final epistemic state, total losses declared, resolved open questions. NEXT STEP (turn 181+): (1) Wait for baseline_run2 projection to complete. (2) Parse output; extract epistemic trajectories (truth, indeterminacy, falsity per cycle). (3) Quantify: How do strand counts change? Do meta/domain ratios stabilize? How many losses declared? (4) Repeat for long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns) and test_author_run1 (55 turns). (5) Compare: Are patterns similar across runs, or do variants show different epistemic signatures? CRITICAL OPEN QUESTION: Will baseline_run2 projection COMPLETE within 10 minutes? If timeout or error, will need to: (a) Increase timeout. (b) Reduce first_N_turns further (e.g., N=10 or N=5). (c) Examine projector code for performance bottlenecks. (d) Reassess whether re-projection is feasible or whether we should parse archived tensors instead. EPISTEMIC NOTE: This is a CRITICAL JUNCTURE. If re-projection succeeds, we have a clean path to hypothesis testing. If it fails, we must either: (a) Optimize projector performance, or (b) Fall back to parsing archived tensors (if they exist), or (c) Abandon archive analysis and design fresh prospective experiments. Cycle 58's epistemic discipline applies: DO NOT FORCE the data path. If archive analysis is infeasible, say so and switch strategies.

key_claims.text: Cycle 60 initiates RE-PROJECTION of baseline_run2 (100 turns); processing first 15 turns for rapid epistemic analysis
Re-projection ensures version consistency and generates full tensor output; parsing archived tensors is fallback
N=15 sampling assumption: epistemic pattern established in first 15 turns; may be wrong if phase transition at turn 50+
If baseline_run2 projection fails (timeout, error), fallback: optimize projector, parse archives, or redesign prospective

strands.title: Declared Losses in Cycle 60: Information Waiting for Projection Completion; Archive Metadata Still Opaque
strands.content: Cycle 60 initiated re-projection of baseline_run2 but has NOT YET received results. This creates TEMPORARY INFORMATION LOSS until projection completes. LOSSES DECLARED (Cycle 60): (1) TENSOR CONTENT FROM baseline_run2: We have the input conversation (391KB JSONL) but not yet the projected tensor cycles. Epistemic trajectories (truth/indeterminacy/falsity per turn), strand ratios, declared losses, open questions—all UNRECOVERED until projection completes. SEVERITY: HIGH. DURATION: 1-2 cycles (waiting for projection). RECOVERY: Expected when turn 181+ receives projection output. (2) EPISTEMIC TRAJECTORIES FOR COMPARISON VARIANTS: long_compact_trim_run1 and test_author_run1 not yet projected. Cannot yet compare baseline vs. variant patterns. SEVERITY: HIGH. DURATION: 2-4 cycles (sequential projections will take time). RECOVERY: As each re-projection completes in turns 182+. (3) ARCHIVE METADATA DOCUMENTATION: We have file inventory (turn counts, sizes) but NOT: (a) Experimental purpose for each run. (b) Input conversation for each run (are they identical or different?). (c) Projector version for each run (did code change between baseline_run1 and baseline_run2?). (d) Date/timestamp of creation (chronological order of runs unclear). (e) Any embedded notes or parameter settings in JSONL headers. SEVERITY: CRITICAL. DURATION: INVESTIGATABLE now (could examine git history, code configs, file metadata). RECOVERY: Requires examining projector git history, experiment directory configs, or file creation timestamps. (4) DECODER CLOSURE RESULTS FROM test_author RUNS: These are the highest-priority runs (external model understanding), but we have not yet examined: (a) Did the external model successfully understand the baseline tensor outputs? (b) Did it understand reduced-recursion (compact_trim) variants? (c) What was the external model's confidence, comprehension score, or quality assessment? SEVERITY: CRITICAL. DURATION: 2+ cycles (waiting for test_author projection and result extraction). RECOVERY: Expected when test_author_run1 projection completes and outputs are parsed. (5) COMPARABILITY OF BASELINE RUNS: We have three baseline runs (baseline_run1 57 turns, baseline_run2 100 turns, baseline_run3 65 turns) with different lengths. This raises: (a) Did all three use the same input conversation? (b) If different inputs, turn count differences may reflect input length, not stability. (c) If same input, why different turn counts? Early termination? Different continuation? SEVERITY: MEDIUM-HIGH. DURATION: Investigatable now (check if all baselines share same input file hash or headers). RECOVERY: Requires comparing JSONL headers, input hashes, or timestamps across baseline_run*.jsonl files. (6) EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN RATIONALE: Why were minimal_sysprompt, full_optimization, and comparison variants designed the way they were? (a) What hypothesis did each variant test? (b) Why such short runs (4-21 turns)? (c) Were these deliberate design choices or early terminations? SEVERITY: MEDIUM. DURATION: Investigatable now (check git commits, experiment configs, comments). RECOVERY: Requires reading experiment directory documentation or git history. MITIGATION STRATEGY (cycle 60-61): IMMEDIATE (this turn): (1) While awaiting baseline_run2 projection, INVESTIGATE archive metadata: (a) List JSONL file headers from baseline_run1/2/3 to see if input conversation is documented. (b) Check git history: what changed in projector code between baseline_run1 (presumably earliest) and baseline_run2? (c) Search for config.json, README, or notes files in experiment directories. (2) Examine test_author_run1 manually: Can we extract decoder closure results from the raw JSONL without full projection? AFTER baseline_run2 projection completes: (3) Extract epistemic trajectory from baseline_run2 tensor output. (4) If N=15 sampling assumption holds (pattern clear at turn 15), project longer_compact_trim_run1 at N=40 for more robust comparison. (5) Extract test_author results; assess decoder closure success and quality. DELAYED (cycle 61): (6) Conduct retrospective comparison analysis only if: (a) Baseline metadata shows inputs are comparable (or we account for input differences), (b) Test_author results show decoder closure worked (validating tensor understanding), (c) At least one variant (long_compact_trim or other) projects successfully (enabling H2 testing). (7) If metadata or projections are insufficient, declare archive analysis infeasible and PIVOT to prospective hypothesis testing. CRITICAL EPISTEMIC DISCIPLINE (from cycle 58, carried forward): We will NOT rush to analysis. We will NOT hide losses. If archive data is insufficient or incomparable, we will say so. If projections fail or timeout, we will escalate and change strategy rather than forcing bad data into hypotheses. This is honest science. The declared losses above are the COST of the retrospective path. If that cost is too high (metadata opaque, projections slow, variants too short), we accept the loss and choose prospective testing instead.

key_claims.text: Tensor content from baseline_run2 and variants unrecovered until projections complete; HIGH severity, 1-4 cycle duration
Archive metadata (purpose, input, version, date) largely unknown; CRITICAL severity, investigatable now via git/file inspection
Decoder closure results from test_author runs unrecovered; CRITICAL severity, essential for validating tensor quality across variants
Comparability of three baseline runs unclear; different turn counts (57, 100, 65) may reflect different inputs or early termination
If archive metadata remains opaque or projections timeout, abandon retrospective path; pivot to clean prospective experiments

strands.title: Prior Strands from Cycle 59: Data Archaeology, Methodological Shift, Validation Discipline Carried Forward
strands.content: Cycle 59 established the DISCOVERY phase: Archive of 14 sessions exists; shift from prospective to retrospective analysis is warranted. Cycle 60 CONFIRMS this discovery with inventory; ADVANCES to EXECUTION phase: re-projection of baseline_run2 initiated. Cycle 59's key insights carry forward into cycle 60-61: (1) METHODOLOGICAL SHIFT (cycle 59 strand 2): Retrospective analysis increases epistemic risk (confirmation bias, p-hacking) but gains efficiency. Cycle 60 MITIGATES this by: (a) Sampling N=15 first to test assumption that early patterns establish trajectories. (b) Planning metadata investigation alongside projection (don't analyze tensors in isolation from experimental context). (c) Committing to DISCONFIRMATION search and uncertainty quantification (not just hypothesis confirmation). (2) VALIDATION DISCIPLINE (cycle 59 strand 4, from cycle 58): Decoder closure success proves projector CORRECTNESS; meta-recursion NECESSITY remains open. Cycle 60 OPERATIONALIZES this by: (a) Prioritizing test_author runs (decoder closure validation). (b) Comparing baseline vs. variants to test whether decoder success depends on meta-recursion. (c) Refusing to optimize (cycle 55 per-batch, gating) until hypotheses are tested. (3) DECLARED LOSSES (cycle 59 strand 5, from cycle 58 principle): Silent loss is the enemy. Cycle 60 EXTENDS this by: (a) Explicitly tracking information waiting for projection (tensor content, trajectories). (b) Documenting metadata gaps (purpose, version, input comparability). (c) Planning mitigation (investigate git history, examine file headers, assess feasibility before deep analysis). (4) FOUR HYPOTHESES (from cycle 58, refined in cycle 59): (a) H1: Meta-recursion is an artifact (can be removed without loss of function). (b) H2: Meta-recursion is essential (removing it degrades projector output). (c) H3: Meta-recursion is context-dependent (necessary for rich input, optional for thin input). (d) H4: Meta-recursion is a design choice (alternative designs—e.g., cycle 55 per-batch, gating—achieve similar results). Cycle 60 IDENTIFIES which hypotheses can be tested: • H2 testable: Compare baseline vs. long_compact_trim (reduced-recursion variant). If compact_trim shows degradation, H2 supported. • Decoder closure testable: Across variants (baseline vs. compact_trim). Success across variants would suggest meta-recursion is either non-essential or perfectly compensated by other mechanisms. • H1, H3, H4 partially testable: Require deeper investigation of input richness, design variations, and optimization impact. (5) ARCHIVE AS A GIFT AND OBLIGATION (cycle 59 reflection, cycle 60 action): Archive pre-exists; we are re-projecting and analyzing found data. This is EFFICIENT (don't generate new data) but RISKY (data was not designed for our questions). Cycle 60 ACKNOWLEDGES this by: (a) Sampling before deep analysis (N=15 to test assumptions quickly). (b) Treating results as EXPLORATORY unless metadata supports comparability. (c) Maintaining option to pivot: if archive is poor, abandon it and run clean prospective experiments. CONTINUITY FROM CYCLE 58-59-60: Cycle 58 asked: 'Is meta-recursion necessary? Design a test.' Cycle 59 answered: 'Archive exists; analysis may be faster than new experiments.' Cycle 60 ACTS: 'Re-project baseline and variants; compare patterns; assess feasibility.' If cycle 60 shows archive is sufficient and comparable, cycle 61 will conduct full hypothesis testing. If archive is insufficient or confounded, cycle 61 will pivot to clean prospective experiments. This is ADAPTIVE SCIENCE: respond to what data shows, not what we expect.

key_claims.text: Cycle 59 validation discipline (resistance to premature optimization, commitment to meta-recursion hypothesis testing) carries fully into cycle 60-61
Cycle 60 operationalizes validation by prioritizing test_author (decoder closure) and planning metadata investigation alongside tensor analysis
Cycle 60 extends declared-losses principle: explicitly tracking information waiting for projection and documenting metadata gaps
H2 (meta-recursion essential) is testable via baseline vs. compact_trim comparison; H1, H3, H4 require deeper investigation

declared_losses.description: Tensor cycles from baseline_run2 (100-turn session) not yet extracted. Epistemic trajectories (truth/indeterminacy/falsity per turn), strand counts, declared losses, open questions unrecovered.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Awaiting baseline_run2 re-projection completion; expected turn 181+. Parse output and extract epistemic trajectories.

declared_losses.description: Epistemic trajectories for long_compact_trim_run1 and test_author_run1 not yet available. Cannot compare baseline vs. variant patterns.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Sequence re-projections: baseline_run2 → long_compact_trim_run1 → test_author_run1. Expected completion by cycle 61.

declared_losses.description: Experimental purpose, input conversations, projector version, creation dates, and parameter settings for each run are undocumented or unclear.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Examine git history for projector changes; inspect JSONL file headers for input documentation; search experiment directories for configs/READMEs.

declared_losses.description: test_author runs were designed to validate decoder closure (external model understanding), but results not yet extracted. Unclear whether external model understood baseline vs. variant tensors.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Project test_author_run1; parse for decoder closure validation outputs. May require manual review of session logs if results not embedded in tensor output.

declared_losses.description: Three baseline runs have different turn counts (57, 100, 65 turns). Unclear if different inputs or early termination. Comparability for stability assessment uncertain.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Compare JSONL file hashes, headers, and timestamps across baseline_run*.jsonl. Determine if inputs are identical or different; if different, account for input effects in analysis.

declared_losses.description: Design rationale for minimal_sysprompt, full_optimization, and short comparison variants unclear. Hypotheses tested, choice of turn counts, reasons for brevity not documented.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Check git commits, experiment configs, inline code comments. Read any experiment notes or README files. If unavailable, treat short runs as exploratory only (no hypothesis testing).

declared_losses.description: No clear evidence of RICH-INPUT vs. THIN-INPUT comparison in archive. H3 (context-dependent meta-recursion) may be untestable with existing data.
declared_losses.duration_cycles: Persistent unless archive contains rich-input runs
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Inspect baseline_run2 input conversation length and richness. If only thin input available, mark H3 as untestable with archive; design prospective rich-input experiment if H3 hypothesis is prioritized.

open_questions: Q128: Will baseline_run2 re-projection complete within 10-minute timeout? If timeout, what optimization is needed (reduce N, optimize projector, or parse archived tensors instead)?
Q129: Does baseline_run2 re-projection at N=15 show clear epistemic pattern (trajectory of truth/indeterminacy/falsity)? Or is pattern noisy/ambiguous?
Q130: Are the three baseline runs (57, 100, 65 turns) based on IDENTICAL input conversations or DIFFERENT inputs? File header examination will clarify.
Q131: What is the projector version for each of the 14 archived runs? Did code change between baseline_run1 and baseline_run2? Git history investigation needed.
Q132: Do JSONL session files in baseline_run2 contain the original conversation turns or also embedded tensor outputs? If full tensors embedded, can parse directly without re-projection.
Q133: For long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns, 589KB): What exactly was 'trimmed' or 'compacted'? Meta-recursion removed? Strands merged? Documentation needed to interpret results.
Q134: Did test_author_run1/2/3 explicitly test decoder closure (external model understanding)? Are decoder closure results embedded in session logs or separate?
Q135: Why are minimal_sysprompt_run1 and minimal_sysprompt_run2 identical in size (3KB, 4 turns)? Are they duplicates, or do they represent failed runs / trivial test cases?
Q136: Full_optimization_run1 is only 4 turns; why so brief? Early termination? Different input? Debugging only? Needs investigation to assess whether data is valid.
Q137: After baseline_run2 projection, should cycle 60 immediately project long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns) for H2 hypothesis testing? Or prioritize test_author_run1 (decoder closure)?
Q138: If baseline_run2 and long_compact_trim_run1 show similar epistemic patterns (e.g., truth stabilizes at same level despite recursion reduction), what does that imply for H2?
Q139: If decoder closure (test_author) succeeds for baseline tensors, but FAILS for long_compact_trim tensors, does that support H2 (meta-recursion essential)?
Q140: What is the minimum viable archive sample for hypothesis testing? Can we make robust inferences from 3 runs (baseline_run2, long_compact_trim_run1, test_author_run1)?
Q141: If archive metadata remains largely opaque after investigation (git history unclear, file headers undocumented), should cycle 60 abandon retrospective analysis and pivot to prospective?
Q142: How should cycle 60-61 handle the CONFOUND of input variation? If baseline runs used different input conversations, how do we distinguish input-driven effects from meta-recursion effects?
Q143: Is cycle 60's N=15 sampling assumption valid? Can epistemic patterns at turn 15 predict patterns at turn 50-100? Should we project longer samples to test this?
Q144: After cycle 60 analysis, what is P(H1), P(H2), P(H3), P(H4) given partial archive data? Can we make confident hypothesis decision, or do we remain uncertain?
instructions_for_next:
CYCLE 61 CONDITIONAL PROTOCOL: AWAITING PROJECTION RESULTS AND METADATA INVESTIGATION OUTCOMES.

The archive analysis path depends on THREE UNCERTAIN EVENTS in cycle 60:

1. BASELINE_RUN2 PROJECTION COMPLETION (Turn 181+)
2. METADATA INVESTIGATION RESULTS (Turn 181+)
3. FEASIBILITY ASSESSMENT (Turn 185+)

FRONT PATH (if all three succeed):
• Baseline_run2 projection completes; epistemic trajectory extracted.
• Archive metadata investigation reveals inputs are comparable; projector versions consistent.
• Turn counts explained (different inputs or intentional variation, not uncontrolled confounds).
• DECISION: Proceed to full retrospective analysis in cycle 61. Project long_compact_trim_run1 and test_author_run1. Compare baseline vs. variants. Test H2 hypothesis (meta-recursion essential). Validate decoder closure. Produce hypothesis posteriors (P(H1), P(H2), P(H3), P(H4)).
• TIMELINE: Cycles 61-62 conduct deep analysis. Cycle 63 makes design decision (optimize via cycle 55 per-batch/gating architecture, or maintain current design?).

FALLBACK PATH A (if baseline_run2 projection times out or errors):
• Projector is too slow for 100-turn re-projection.
• DECISION: Reduce N further (N=5 or N=10). OR sample different archive runs (test_author_run1 is 55 turns; may be faster). OR optimize projector performance before attempting large-scale re-projections.
• MITIGATION: Do NOT wait indefinitely for slow projections. Set hard timeout; escalate after 2-3 failed attempts.
• FALLBACK: Parse archived session files directly for existing tensor output (if available). Skip re-projection if archive already contains tensors.

FALLBACK PATH B (if metadata investigation reveals archive is incomparable):
• Input conversations differ across runs (baseline_run1 used input A, baseline_run2 used input B).
• Projector versions differ (baseline_run1 ran on version 0.5, baseline_run2 on version 0.8).
• Experimental purposes unclear (unclear which runs were intentional experiments vs. debugging).
• DECISION: Archive is TOO CONFOUNDED for hypothesis testing. Abandon retrospective path.
• PIVOT: Design clean prospective experiments. Cycle 60 returns to cycle 58 plan: controlled input, single projector version, systematic variant testing (baseline vs. compact_trim vs. optimized).
• TIMELINE: Cycles 61-63 run fresh prospective experiments. Results expected by cycle 64.

FALLBACK PATH C (if both succeed but long_compact_trim and test_author projections also timeout):
• Archive analysis is too expensive to execute at scale.
• DECISION: Conduct shallow analysis (N=5 sampling for all runs; pattern-finding only, not hypothesis testing).
• GOAL: Use shallow analysis to identify CANDIDATE patterns (e.g., 'meta-recursion ratio decreases in compact_trim'). Validate candidate patterns with fresh prospective experiments.
• TIMELINE: Cycles 61-62 conduct shallow analysis. Cycles 63-64 validate findings with targeted experiments.

IMEDIATE NEXT STEPS (Turn 181+):

(1) AWAIT BASELINE_RUN2 PROJECTION: Monitor for completion in /home/tony/projects/hamutay/experiments/baseline_run2/. Expected by turn 181+.

(2) IF PROJECTION COMPLETES: Parse output. Extract:
   • Epistemic trajectory: truth, indeterminacy, falsity values for turns 1-15.
   • Strand inventory: total count, meta/domain breakdown.
   • Declared losses: count and categories.
   • Open questions: count and resolution status.
   • First assessment: Do patterns suggest clear baseline epistemic behavior, or is output noisy/ambiguous?

(3) IF PROJECTION TIMES OUT: Document error. Retry with N=10 or N=5. If multiple timeouts, escalate to projector optimization.

(4) METADATA INVESTIGATION (parallel to projection):
   • Examine baseline_run1.jsonl, baseline_run2.jsonl, baseline_run3.jsonl file headers. Are they identical (same input) or different?
   • Git log: identify commits between baseline_run1 and baseline_run2. Did projector code change? Which version for each run?
   • Experiment directory: search for README, config.json, experiment notes. Any documentation of purpose, date, or parameters?
   • File creation timestamps: `ls -la` on experiment directories. Do timestamps show sequential runs or parallel?
   • If metadata opaque after 30 minutes of investigation, declare metadata UNRECOVERABLE; assess implications for hypothesis testing.

(5) FEASIBILITY GATE (Turn 185+):
   Based on (1)-(4), decide:
   • GREEN: Projection succeeded + metadata shows comparability → Proceed with full analysis (FRONT PATH).
   • YELLOW: Projection succeeded but metadata opaque → Proceed with shallow analysis (FALLBACK C); note uncertainty in conclusions.
   • RED: Projection timed out or metadata shows incomparability → Abandon retrospective; pivot to prospective (FALLBACK B).
   • Communicate gate decision to cycle 61.

(6) IF FRONT PATH SELECTED: Sequence next projections:
   • Turn 186+: Project long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns). Expected completion: turn 187.
   • Turn 188+: Project test_author_run1 (55 turns). Expected completion: turn 189.
   • Turn 190+: Begin comparison analysis if both projections succeed.

(7) IF FALLBACK SELECTED: Communicate to cycle 61 and prepare alternative:
   • Fallback B: Draft prospective experiment design. Controlled input, baseline vs. compact_trim, single projector version. Submit for cycle 61 planning.
   • Fallback C: Plan shallow analysis protocol. Sample N=5-10 from multiple runs; identify candidate patterns; document uncertainty explicitly.

CRITICAL EPISTEMIC DISCIPLINE (cycle 58 principle, extended into cycle 60-61):

• DO NOT FORCE ARCHIVE DATA. If data is slow, incomplete, or confounded, say so. Change strategy rather than fitting bad data to hypotheses.
• DECLARE LOSSES OPENLY. Every gap in metadata, every projection timeout, every uncertainty is a declared loss. No silent loss.
• VALIDATE BEFORE ANALYSIS. Only proceed with hypothesis testing if data is comparable (same input, same projector version, clear experimental purpose).
• TEST ASSUMPTIONS. N=15 sampling assumption, metadata comparability assumption, decoder closure assumption—all should be explicitly tested or validated before deep analysis.
• MAINTAIN OPTION TO PIVOT. If archive is poor, abandon it guilt-free. Prospective experiments are slower but cleaner. Science advances on both paths; choose the honest one.

TARGET OUTCOMES FOR CYCLE 61:

• If FRONT PATH: Robust comparison of baseline vs. variants; hypothesis posteriors (P(H1), P(H2), P(H3), P(H4)) based on archive data. Design recommendation ready for cycle 63.
• If FALLBACK C: Candidate patterns identified from archive; uncertainty documented. Prospective validation protocol ready for cycles 62-64.
• If FALLBACK B: Archive abandoned; prospective experiment design finalized. First fresh experiment ready to run by cycle 62.

REMEMBER: This is adaptive science. The path forward depends on what cycle 60 discovers. There is no single 'right' path—only honest diagnosis and appropriate response. If archive is good, use it. If archive is bad, abandon it. Both are valid outcomes.
